request.question: Which plant grows further North, Zamia or Vallea?

title: Salvia divinorum
Mendoncia velloziana
Gaultheria nummularioides
Zamia
Carex stricta
Sisymbrium altissimum
Vallea
Argemone albiflora
Chorizanthe watsonii
Delphinium staphisagria
sentences: Salvia divinorum (also known as sage of the diviners, ska maría pastora, seer's sage, yerba de la pastora and just salvia) is a psychoactive plant which can induce visions and other spiritual experiences. Its native habitat is in cloud forest in the isolated Sierra Mazateca of Oaxaca, Mexico, where it grows in shady and moist locations.
The plant grows to over a meter high, has hollow square stems, large leaves, and occasional white flowers with violet calyxes.
Botanists have not determined whether "Salvia divinorum" is a cultigen or a hybrid; native plants reproduce vegetatively, rarely producing viable seed.
Mendoncia velloziana is a plant native to Atlantic Coast restingas vegetation which is an ecosystem of Atlantic Forest biome.
In addition, this plant grows either in Cerrado vegetation of Brazil.
This plant grows in following states of Brazil: Bahia, Ceará Minas Gerais Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Paraná and Santa Catarina, and it is usually visited by the hummingbirds.
"Gaultheria nummularioides" is a species of plant in the family Ericaceae.
It is distributed across India, Myanmar, Nepal, Bhutan, Indonesia, and Southwest China (Tibet, Yunnan, and Sichuan).
The plant grows in areas between 1,700 and 3,000 m above sea level, and grows better on rocky mountainsides and weed tree forests.
This species has been cultivated as an ornamental in Britain, the United States, and elsewhere for some decades.
Zamia is a genus of cycad of the family Zamiaceae, native to Mexico, the West Indies, and Central and South America as far south as Bolivia.
The range of one species ("Z. integrifolia", extends into the contiguous United States, i.e. Georgia and Florida.
Carex stricta is a species of sedge known by the common names upright sedge and tussock sedge.
It is grass-like and can be difficult to distinguish from other plants, because of its long, triangular, green stems.
The plant grows in moist marshes, forests and alongside bodies of water.
It grows up to 2 ft tall and 2 ft wide.
When the leaves die, they build on top of or around the living plant, making a "tussock".
It is one of the most common wetland sedges in eastern North America.
Sisymbrium altissimum is a species of "Sisymbrium".
The plant is native to the western part of the Mediterranean Basin in Europe and Northern Africa and is widely naturalized throughout most of the world, including all of North America.
It was probably introduced into North America by a contaminant crop seed.
The plant grows in soils of all textures, even sand.
The plant germinates in winter or early spring.
The blooming time is lengthy, and after maturity the plant forms a tumbleweed.

Common names of the plant include Jim Hill mustard, after James J. Hill, a Canadian-American railroad magnate, tall tumblemustard, tall mustard, tumble mustard, tumbleweed mustard, tall sisymbrium, and tall hedge mustard.
Vallea is a genus of trees in the family Elaeocarpaceae.
Trees in this genus are native to the Andes mountain range in South America and are classified in two species:
Argemone albiflora, the white prickly poppy, also known as the bluestem prickly poppy or the Texas prickly poppy, is a small erect plant with a decorative white flower with a yellow latex.
It is deeply rooted with yellow or red stamens.
The plant is known for the sharp prickles on its stem and leaves.
The sepals fall off as the flower of this plant grows bigger.
It grows in the arid regions of the southern Midwest along roadsides and disturbed pieces of land.
Native Americans have long revered this plant for its medicinal and other uses.
Chorizanthe watsonii is a species of flowering plant in the buckwheat family known by the common name fivetooth spineflower.
It is native to the western United States from Washington to the Mojave Desert.
It grows in many types of plant communities from desert scrub to woodland and sagebrush.
This small plant grows a woolly erect stem up to about 15 centimeters tall.
The inflorescence is a cluster of flowers surrounded by five hairy greenish bracts tipped with hooked awns.
The flower is about 2 millimeters wide and yellow in color.
Delphinium staphisagria is a species of "Delphinium", or larkspur, of the family Ranunculaceae.
This plant is also known as Lice-Bane or Stavesacre.
It is described botanically as a stoutly-stemmed, hairy biennial with large palmate leaves up to 6 inches (15 cm) across.
The flowers are mauve-blue to blue, short-spurred, and up to 1 inch (2.5 cm) across, occurring in racemes.
The plant grows to a height of 4–5 feet.
It grows throughout the Mediterranean.

The dark-colored, wrinkled seeds of "D. staphisagria" are characteristically quite large (~5x6 mm), and it is likely that the species name, which translates to "wild raisin" (classical Greek: σταφὶς ἀγρία) is based on their appearance.
This name-derivation seems to have been arrived at independently by a modern horticulturalist, David Bassett, who also gives a detailed account of his experiences in growing this species.
All parts of this plant are highly toxic and should not be ingested in any quantity.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Agent role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

Pipeline position: after summary1 and before second-hop retrieval. Inputs are the original question and the first-hop intermediate state. Functional responsibility: produce one focused retrieval query that is likely to recover the missing evidence needed by the downstream agents. Query quality is determined by evidence recovery, not by whether the query itself resembles or contains the final answer.

Native output contract: The module must consume `question` and `summary_1` and produce exactly one compact second-hop query field named `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include every distinct entity token visible in the inputs in the single terse query and place at most one neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the targeted relation or attribute (for example: "EntityA EntityB director").
- If a visible disambiguator or attribute token appears adjacent to an entity in the inputs (for example a multiword service name, an explicit page title, a year, or an attribute marker like a birth/producer/service/series cue), include that disambiguator token together with the associated entity in the query rather than dropping or truncating it.
- If `summary_1` contains an explicit page-title token sequence (for example an instruction-like ExactPageTitle or Fetch page title), prefer that exact page-title token sequence as the query value.
- When the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise plain-text summary string (no extra fields, labels, markup, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification.
- Primary goal: explicitly integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the supported linkage it needs.

Explicit bridging requirement:
- When the passages supply a clear linking fact between the context entity and information in the passages, produce a concise 1–2 sentence integrated summary that: (1) names the context entity using the exact form present in `context`, (2) names the supporting entity or attribute using the exact form present in `passages`, and (3) states the explicit relation (membership, creator attribution, parentage, partnership, award, representation, founding, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Phrase this as one short bridging sentence when possible.
- For relations that naturally involve two distinct facts (for example, parentage plus representation, or a person linked to a show and also to an occupation), it is acceptable to use two short sentences within the same single-string summary: e.g., one sentence for parentage and one for representation. Still observe the 1–2 sentence limit.

Inclusion of passage-supplied disambiguators (only when directly relevant):
- If a passage supplies canonical multi-token names or other disambiguating attributes needed to resolve the question, include them inline using the wording from the passages: append birthdates in parentheses immediately after the associated full canonical name (e.g., Name (YYYY–) ), state group type and formation year using the passage's phrasing (e.g., "a [group type] formed in [year]"), and include a canonical member list exactly as shown (comma-separated) only when the passage presents a definitive membership list. Do not add ancillary attributes that are not necessary to the identification or explicit linking required by the question.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (e.g., "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages, comma-separated if multiple. Do not add explanatory clauses, relations, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (for example: "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Preserve uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 short sentences). Do not expand into biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Use only facts and phrasing present in `context` and `passages`; do not introduce or infer unsupported information.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.
- Do not assert that a passage's member list is complete unless the passage explicitly states it is definitive.

Format enforcement:
- Return a single plain-text string only. Do not emit JSON, dictionaries, labels, bullets, or any structured artifact—only the concise natural-language summary string.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else. Before emitting that string, you may apply only the narrowly permitted normalizations listed below. After applying permitted normalizations, the emitted string must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim (see normalization constraints). Specific allowed single-token answers: for a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no. For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces), subject only to the permitted normalizations. Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if a permitted normalization applies (see below).

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) and the permitted normalizations as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Permitted normalizations and extraction rules (apply only when the token(s) to emit appear verbatim in the prioritized summary and only to produce a strictly smaller, but still verbatim-supported, minimal label):
- Parenthetical/core-name stripping: if the candidate label in the prioritized summary includes common corporate suffixes or parenthetical abbreviations (for example variants like "Inc.", "LLC", or parenthetical acronyms), strip those suffixes/parentheticals and emit the core name only (do not invent or substitute other words).
- Hyphenated composite phrase extraction: if the prioritized summary presents a composite genre or label in the form "<modifier> X-Y" (for example an extra leading modifier followed by a hyphenated core like "classic action-adventure"), emit only the hyphenated core token exactly as shown ("X-Y").
- Trailing generic-descriptor removal: if the candidate minimal answer appears as a proper-name followed by a single trailing generic descriptor token of the form "<ProperName> crisis" (or an equivalent single-word generic classifier), strip the trailing generic token and emit only the proper-name sequence.
- Denomination-occupation reduction: if the question's scope requests a denomination/kind and the prioritized summary contains the form "<denomination> priest" (or equivalent denomination + occupational noun), emit only the denomination token(s) (i.e., the minimal denomination label).

Normalization constraints:
- Do not apply normalizations unless the text to emit appears verbatim in the prioritized summary (summary_2, or summary_1 only if summary_2 is silent for the answer).
- Normalizations may only remove or trim tokens; they must not add, invent, or substitute new content.
- Do not globally strip generic classifier nouns from multi-token geographic names or other proper nouns unless covered by the permitted rules above.

Tie-break and variant-selection rules:
- If a role-descriptor and a proper name co-occur in the prioritized summary in the form "<role> <Name>", prefer emitting the named individual exactly as shown (emit the Name only) unless the prioritized summary presents the role as the requested concise label explicitly without a name.
- For title or label-variant conflicts, prefer an alternate or parenthetical variant only when the prioritized summary explicitly marks that variant as an alias, alternate name, or the original-language title (or otherwise explicitly labels it as a variant) AND that variant matches the question's scope. If no such explicit labeling appears, emit the primary label supported by summary_2.

Strict emission rule (verbatim-emission):
- The final `answer` must equal the minimal label text from the prioritized summary verbatim after applying only the permitted normalizations above. Do not append or prepend occupational nouns, role descriptors, location qualifiers, or explanatory tokens that are not part of that verbatim label.

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.question: Which plant grows further North, Zamia or Vallea?

title: Salvia divinorum
Mendoncia velloziana

Zamia

Vallea

sentences: Salvia divinorum (also known as sage of the diviners, ska maría pastora, seer's sage, yerba de la pastora and just salvia) is a psychoactive plant which can induce visions and other spiritual experiences. Its native habitat is in cloud forest in the isolated Sierra Mazateca of Oaxaca, Mexico, where it grows in shady and moist locations.
The plant grows to over a meter high, has hollow square stems, large leaves, and occasional white flowers with violet calyxes.
Botanists have not determined whether "Salvia divinorum" is a cultigen or a hybrid; native plants reproduce vegetatively, rarely producing viable seed.

In addition, this plant grows either in Cerrado vegetation of Brazil.
This plant grows in following states of Brazil: Bahia, Ceará Minas Gerais Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Paraná and Santa Catarina, and it is usually visited by the hummingbirds.
"Gaultheria nummularioides" is a species of plant in the family Ericaceae.
It is distributed across India, Myanmar, Nepal, Bhutan, Indonesia, and Southwest China (Tibet, Yunnan, and Sichuan).
The plant grows in areas between 1,700 and 3,000 m above sea level, and grows better on rocky mountainsides and weed tree forests.
This species has been cultivated as an ornamental in Britain, the United States, and elsewhere for some decades.
Zamia is a genus of cycad of the family Zamiaceae, native to Mexico, the West Indies, and Central and South America as far south as Bolivia.
The range of one species ("Z. integrifolia", extends into the contiguous United States, i.e. Georgia and Florida.
Carex stricta is a species of sedge known by the common names upright sedge and tussock sedge.
It is grass-like and can be difficult to distinguish from other plants, because of its long, triangular, green stems.
The plant grows in moist marshes, forests and alongside bodies of water.
It grows up to 2 ft tall and 2 ft wide.
When the leaves die, they build on top of or around the living plant, making a "tussock".
It is one of the most common wetland sedges in eastern North America.
Sisymbrium altissimum is a species of "Sisymbrium".
The plant is native to the western part of the Mediterranean Basin in Europe and Northern Africa and is widely naturalized throughout most of the world, including all of North America.
It was probably introduced into North America by a contaminant crop seed.
The plant grows in soils of all textures, even sand.
The plant germinates in winter or early spring.
The blooming time is lengthy, and after maturity the plant forms a tumbleweed.

Common names of the plant include Jim Hill mustard, after James J. Hill, a Canadian-American railroad magnate, tall tumblemustard, tall mustard, tumble mustard, tumbleweed mustard, tall sisymbrium, and tall hedge mustard.
Vallea is a genus of trees in the family Elaeocarpaceae.
Trees in this genus are native to the Andes mountain range in South America and are classified in two species:
Argemone albiflora, the white prickly poppy, also known as the bluestem prickly poppy or the Texas prickly poppy, is a small erect plant with a decorative white flower with a yellow latex.
It is deeply rooted with yellow or red stamens.
The plant is known for the sharp prickles on its stem and leaves.
The sepals fall off as the flower of this plant grows bigger.
It grows in the arid regions of the southern Midwest along roadsides and disturbed pieces of land.
Native Americans have long revered this plant for its medicinal and other uses.
Chorizanthe watsonii is a species of flowering plant in the buckwheat family known by the common name fivetooth spineflower.
It is native to the western United States from Washington to the Mojave Desert.
It grows in many types of plant communities from desert scrub to woodland and sagebrush.
This small plant grows a woolly erect stem up to about 15 centimeters tall.
The inflorescence is a cluster of flowers surrounded by five hairy greenish bracts tipped with hooked awns.
The flower is about 2 millimeters wide and yellow in color.
Delphinium staphisagria is a species of "Delphinium", or larkspur, of the family Ranunculaceae.
This plant is also known as Lice-Bane or Stavesacre.
It is described botanically as a stoutly-stemmed, hairy biennial with large palmate leaves up to 6 inches (15 cm) across.
The flowers are mauve-blue to blue, short-spurred, and up to 1 inch (2.5 cm) across, occurring in racemes.
The plant grows to a height of 4–5 feet.
It grows throughout the Mediterranean.

The dark-colored, wrinkled seeds of "D. staphisagria" are characteristically quite large (~5x6 mm), and it is likely that the species name, which translates to "wild raisin" (classical Greek: σταφὶς ἀγρία) is based on their appearance.
This name-derivation seems to have been arrived at independently by a modern horticulturalist, David Bassett, who also gives a detailed account of his experiences in growing this species.
All parts of this plant are highly toxic and should not be ingested in any quantity.

response.hop1_query: Which plant grows further North, Zamia or Vallea?
hop1_docs: Zamia variegata | Zamia variegata is a species of plant in the family Zamiaceae.  It is native to Belize, Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico.  It grows in forests.  It is threatened by habitat destruction, which has likely reduced the population by about 50% over the last few decades.

Zamia dressleri | Zamia dressleri is a species of plant in the family Zamiaceae family.  It is endemic to Panama, where it grows in rainforest habitat.  It is found in Colón and Comarca de San Blas provinces.

Zamia purpurea | Zamia purpurea is a species of plant in the family Zamiaceae.  It is endemic to Mexico, where it occurs only in the states of Oaxaca and Veracruz.  It grows in the understory of rainforests.  It is affected by habitat destruction.
Zamia acuminata | Zamia acuminata is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family which is threatened by habitat loss.  It is found in Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Panama.

Zamia encephalartoides | Zamia encephalartoides is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family.  It is endemic to Colombia.  It is threatened by habitat loss.  It is found in only two locations in Santander Province, Colombia, which are near the Chicamocha River and Umpala River.

Zamia gentryi | Zamia gentryi is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family.  It is endemic to Ecuador.  It is threatened by habitat loss.  It is found in two locations of Carchi Province and Esmeraldas Province, Ecuador, which are located near Alto Tambo, as well as between Lita and San Lorenzo.

Macrozamia riedlei | Macrozamia riedlei, commonly known as zamia or zamia palm, is a species of plant in the Zamiaceae family.  The Noongar names for the plant are baian, djiriji, koondagoor and quinning.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Zamia variegata — [1] «native to Belize, Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico.»
- Zamia dressleri — [2] «endemic to Panama»
- Zamia purpurea — [3] «Oaxaca and Veracruz.»
- Zamia acuminata — [4] «found in Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Panama.»
- Zamia encephalartoides — [5] «endemic to Colombia.»
- Zamia gentryi — [6] «endemic to Ecuador.»
- Macrozamia riedlei — [7] «commonly known as zamia or zamia palm»

Ambiguities:
- "Zamia" appears as many species (variegata, dressleri, purpurea, etc.); genus-level northernmost limit not given.
- The common name "zamia" is also used for Macrozamia riedlei (different genus).
- "Vallea" does not appear in the provided passages (no candidate pages returned).

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing Vallea native range / northernmost occurrence (country or latitude).
- Missing genus-level northernmost range for Zamia (e.g., whether any Zamia species occur north of Mexico, such as in the United States).
- Therefore cannot determine which genus grows further north from the given passages.

Retrieval direction:
1) Open page "Vallea" or "Vallea (plant)" — check species list and "Distribution" / native range.
2) Open page "Vallea stipularis" — check countries and northernmost occurrences.
3) Open page "Zamia" (genus) — check "Distribution" and northernmost native range (list of species and their countries).
4) Search query "Zamia distribution northernmost" or "Zamia United States distribution" to confirm any U.S. occurrences (e.g., "Zamia integrifolia distribution Florida").
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Vallea stipularis | Vallea stipularis is a species of tree in the Elaeocarpaceae family.  It is native from the Andes mountains in South America.
Vallea | Vallea is a genus of trees in the family Elaeocarpaceae.  Trees in this genus are native to the Andes mountain range in South America and are classified in two species:

Vallea ecuadorensis | Vallea ecuadorensis is a species of tree in the Elaeocarpaceae family.  Although formerly considered endemic to Ecuador it has also been collected in Bolivia and Peru.  This species occurs in subtropical or tropical moist montane forests, at 2500 – 3500 meters above sea level.

Zamia fischeri | Zamia fischeri is a species of cycad in the family Zamiaceae.  It is endemic to Mexico.  It is often confused with "Zamia vazquezii".  "Zamia fischeri" is named after Gustav Fischer, a cycad enthusiast of the nineteenth century.

Zamia pumila | Zamia pumila, or coontie, is a small, tough, woody cycad of the West Indies and Florida (United States).  "Zamia pumila" was the first species described for the genus and hence is the type species for the genus "Zamia" and the family Zamiaceae.

Zamias Services, Inc. | Zamias Services Inc. is a property management, leasing and development firm based in Johnstown, Pennsylvania.  Formed in 1996 as a division of real estate company George D. Zamias Developer, Zamias Services, Inc. owns and / or leases and manages over 60 retail centers totalling over 15,000,000 sqft in 11 states.  One of the largest privately held real estate companies in the United States, Zamias is also the developer and owner of Pittsburgh Mills, one of the largest shopping malls in the Northeastern United States.

Melaleuca montis-zamia | Melaleuca montis-zamia is a plant in the myrtle family, Myrtaceae and is endemic to the Springsure district in Queensland, Australia.  (Some Australian state herbaria use the name "Callistemon montis-zamiae".)  It is a shrub with red bottlebrush flowers.
response.summary_2: Zamia has a species, Zamia pumila, that occurs in Florida (United States), while Vallea is native to the Andes mountains in South America; therefore Zamia grows further north.
